topic_description: novel real-time rendering techniques for mobile GPUs with limited bandwidth and compute

raw_idea.Problem: Mobile GPUs use tile-based rendering architectures but apply uniform shading rates across all tiles, wasting compute on low-complexity regions while undersampling high-detail areas.
raw_idea.Existing Methods: Variable Rate Shading (VRS) on desktop GPUs allows per-tile shading rate control, but mobile implementations are limited.
raw_idea.Motivation: By analyzing per-tile content complexity at minimal cost using the previous frame's G-buffer, we can dynamically assign shading rates that concentrate compute on visually important regions.
raw_idea.Proposed Method: A lightweight tile complexity analysis pass computes scores from depth discontinuities, normal variation, and motion vectors. Low-complexity tiles use coarse shading while high-complexity tiles get full-rate shading, with temporal smoothing to avoid flickering.
raw_idea.Experiment Plan: Test on mobile devices using standard scenes. Compare against uniform shading and simple VRS. Measure FPS, power consumption, PSNR, and SSIM.

full_experiment_plan.Title: Adaptive Tile-Based Variable Rate Shading for Energy-Efficient Mobile Rendering
full_experiment_plan.Problem Statement: Mobile GPUs use tile-based rendering architectures but apply uniform shading rates across all tiles, wasting compute on low-complexity regions while undersampling high-detail areas, leading to suboptimal power-performance tradeoffs.
full_experiment_plan.Motivation: Variable Rate Shading on desktop GPUs allows per-tile shading rate control but mobile implementations are limited. By analyzing per-tile content complexity at minimal cost and leveraging the tile-based architecture's on-chip memory, we can dynamically assign shading rates that concentrate compute on visually important regions.
full_experiment_plan.Proposed Method: A lightweight tile complexity analysis pass that runs before the main shading pass. For each tile, compute a complexity score based on depth discontinuities, normal variation, and temporal motion vectors. Low-complexity tiles use coarse shading, high-complexity tiles use full-rate shading, with temporal stability to prevent flickering.

Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 1: Implement Tile Complexity Analys: Compute shader reads previous frame's depth and normal buffers, computes per-tile complexity scores using depth variance, normal variance, and motion magnitude. Use 16x16 tile size matching common mobile GPU tiles.
Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 2: Implement Shading Rate Assignmen: Map complexity scores to rates: <0.2 -> 4x4 coarse, 0.2-0.5 -> 2x2 coarse, >=0.5 -> full-rate. Use Vulkan VK_KHR_fragment_shading_rate or Metal variable rasterization rate.
Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 3: Add Temporal Stability: Exponential moving average on complexity scores (alpha=0.3). Limit rate changes to one step per frame per tile.
Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 4: Prepare Test Scenes: Sponza (moderate), Bistro (high complexity), outdoor terrain. Render at 1080p.
Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 5: Test on Devices: Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 (Adreno 740), Dimensity 9200 (Mali-G715), Apple A17 Pro.
Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 6: Baselines: Uniform full-rate, fixed 2x2 coarse everywhere, luminance-gradient VRS.
Step-by-Step Experiment Plan.Step 7: Measure: FPS, frame time breakdown, bandwidth, power (10-min sessions), PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS vs reference.

Test Case Examples.Baseline: Sponza on Snapdragon 8 Gen 2, uniform full-rate: 45 FPS, 2.8W GPU power.
Test Case Examples.Proposed: Same scene, adaptive VRS: ~40% tiles at 4x4 (walls), ~30% at 2x2 (floors), ~30% full-rate (edges). Expected: 62 FPS, 1.9W, PSNR 38.5 dB.
Test Case Examples.Explanation: Adaptive method identifies low-complexity regions and reduces shading rate without visible quality loss.

full_experiment_plan.Fallback Plan: If adaptive VRS doesn't achieve meaningful savings, analyze which complexity features are most predictive, explore perceptual-aware metrics, or publish as an analysis paper studying tile complexity vs optimal shading rates across mobile architectures.
